title: Free-form fruit galettes
subtitle: Rustic French pastry tarts, folded not tinned
excerpt: A galette is a free-form pastry tart assembled directly on a baking tray: the pastry is rolled, the fruit arranged in the centre, and the edges folded in roughly by hand. No tin, no blind-baking, no crumble topping. The crust is thick, buttery, and slightly crisp. The method works with almost any fruit and is one of the most forgiving pastry recipes.
sourceNotes: The galette rustique is a staple of French domestic baking, documented in regional French cookery throughout the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Unlike the tart, which requires a tin and blind-baking, the galette is assembled directly on the baking surface and relies on the fold of the pastry edge to contain the filling. It is associated with farmhouse and country cooking where informal presentation was standard. The technique of adding a layer of almond cream or ground almonds under the fruit, to absorb juice, appears widely in French regional versions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry can be made 2 days ahead and refrigerated. Assemble and bake on the day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 30 to 38 minutes until the pastry is deep gold and the fruit is tender and juicing

content.text: The key to a galette that does not leak is a layer of ground almonds spread under the fruit before folding. The almonds absorb the juice as the fruit cooks and prevent the pastry from becoming soggy. A tablespoon or two of caster sugar over the fruit before the crust is folded in, and a brush of egg wash on the pastry, gives a properly caramelised result. The method works with apples, pears, plums, apricots, peaches, or berries.

items.prepNote: or other seasonal fruit, peeled and sliced

content.text: Rub 150 g cold diced butter into 250 g plain flour, 25 g caster sugar, and a quarter teaspoon of salt until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs with some larger pieces of butter still visible. Add 4 tablespoons of cold water one at a time, mixing with a fork until the dough just comes together. Form into two discs, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll each pastry disc to a rough circle about 30 cm across and transfer to the trays. Scatter 25 g ground almonds over each, leaving a 4 cm border. Arrange the sliced fruit over the almonds and scatter with caster sugar. Fold the border up and over the edge of the fruit, pressing gently to hold. Brush the pastry with beaten egg. Bake for 30 to 38 minutes until the pastry is deep gold and the fruit is tender. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.problem: Juice leaked out and burnt on the tray
items.solution: The ground almonds layer was too thin or omitted. Make sure the layer covers the full area under the fruit. Stone fruits particularly need a thicker almond layer as they release more juice.
Press the folded pastry edge firmly against the fruit, or use a little beaten egg as a glue where it folds over itself. Chilling the assembled galette for 10 minutes before baking also helps it hold.
